catalog.title: Writing · Requests that do not sound rude
catalog.description: Requests that do not sound rude · A2 writing in “Housing & neighbourhood”: Dutch you can use the same week, guided steps, and a short check at the end.

pedagogy.objective: By the end of this lesson, you can write a short practical message about housing & neighbourhood using a model, gaps, and a final checklist
can_do_outcomes: Fill in basic fields on a housing-related form with support
Describe rooms, furniture, and rental basics (huur, borg) in simple Dutch
Write a short practical message with a clear request.

grammar_points.point: prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)
examples_nl: Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer.
Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw.
examples_en: There is a small leak in the bathroom.
There are two bike sheds by the block.

examples_nl: Jouw sleutel ligt op de keukentafel.
examples_en: Your key is on the kitchen table.

examples_nl: De buren wonen boven ons appartement.
Ik bel de verhuurder over het plafond.
examples_en: The neighbours live above our flat.
I'm calling the landlord about the ceiling.

micro_outcomes: I can recognise key words about housing & neighbourhood in short Dutch texts or audio.
I can copy at least one useful chunk into a sentence about myself.
I can finish most guided tasks in the lesson without peeking at translations on the second try.
I can pass the quick self-check at the end of the lesson.
I know what to revise tomorrow in two minutes or less.

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Write a short practical message about housing & neighbourhood using a model, gaps, and a final checklist

**Grammar thread today:** Er is / er zijn + place — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Housing & neighbourhood**.

Words you might use today:
• **huur**
• **borg**
• **sleutel**
• **buren**
• **lift**
• **kelder**
• **dak**
• **lek**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.example_response: Gisteren belde ik de verhuurder over een klein probleem. Daarna deed ik boodschappen in de buurt en ruimde ik de keuken op.

steps.activity:
**Model message (Dutch)**

Subject: Vraag over bon

Goedemiddag,

Gisteren heb ik boodschappen gedaan bij uw winkel. Ik mis de bon in mijn tas. Kunt u een kopie mailen?

Met vriendelijke groet,
[jouw naam]

**Notice:** polite opening, short problem, clear request, closing.

**Later you will write:** Schrijf 5 zinnen over je buurt: wat vind je fijn, wat wil je verbeteren?

**Today's grammar — *er* + place**

**Rule:** *Er is / er zijn* introduces existence; prepositions pin location (*in, op, naast*).

**Patterns:**
• *Er is een lift.* · *Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen.* · *De sleutel ligt op tafel.*

**Produce:**
1. Describe your home with *Er is …* / *Er zijn …*.
2. Add one place preposition (*naast de deur*).

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — v / f:** *vandaag, vier* — light *v*, not English *w*.

**Language focus — patterns you can reuse**

**prepositions of place (naast, boven, achter, tussen)**
• NL: Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer. / Er zijn twee fietsenstallingen bij het flatgebouw.
• EN: There is a small leak in the bathroom. / There are two bike sheds by the block.

**er + locative (er is, er zijn)**
• NL: Onze huur is inclusief servicekosten. / Jouw sleutel ligt op de keukentafel.
• EN: Our rent includes service charges. / Your key is on the kitchen table.

**possessive pronouns (mijn, jouw, zijn/haar, ons)**
• NL: De buren wonen boven ons appartement. / Ik bel de verhuurder over het plafond.
• EN: The neighbours live above our flat. / I'm calling the landlord about the ceiling.

**Try this:** pick one Dutch sentence above and change only the time or the person (ik → wij / vandaag → morgen), **or** do the “Produce” task from the grammar box above.

**1 — Label the parts:** opening / problem / request / closing in the model email.

**2 — Gap-fill:** Met vriendelijke ______, [naam].

**3 — Politeness:** choose softer Dutch: **Ik wil** → **Ik zou graag willen** in one sentence.

**4 — Combine:** Link with **want**: *Ik bel morgen, want ik ben vandaag druk.*

**5 — Shorten:** remove three words without losing the message.

**6 — Checklist tick:** subject line clear? closing present?

**7 — Your details:** replace [naam] and add one real detail from your life.

**8 — Read aloud:** read your draft slowly.
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Er is een kleine lek in de badkamer.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **huur** and fits *Er is / er zijn + place*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Writing task**

Schrijf 5 zinnen over je buurt: wat vind je fijn, wat wil je verbeteren?

**Phrase bank:**
• huur
• borg
• sleutel
• buren
• lift
• kelder
• dak
• lek
• klusjesman
• huisregels
• afvalscheiding
• parkeerplaats

**Checklist before you finish:**
• Clear subject or first line
• Polite greeting
• Short paragraphs
• **Met vriendelijke groet** + name

**Quick self-check**

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

**Tip:** Say Dutch options out loud if that helps.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.
steps.teacher_notes: Rendered with interactive UI when interaction is present.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **huur**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag huur met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Real-life task · Polite endings that land well** — we keep building **Housing & neighbourhood** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Huisregels** and **rust na 22:00** appear in many flats; neighbours often solve small issues with a short chat before involving the landlord. A calm *Goedemorgen* in the stairwell goes a long way.

**Phrases — landlord & neighbours**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ik wil een storing melden.* | I want to report a fault. |
| *Kunt u kijken naar het plafond?* | Could you look at the ceiling? |
| *Tot welke tijd is het stil in het portiek?* | Until what time is it quiet in the stairwell? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Buur:** Goedemorgen, ik hoorde water in de badkamer.
**Jij:** O nee, ik bel de verhuurder en stuur u een foto.
**Buur:** Dank je, ik ben tot vijf uur thuis.

**Say or write:** One polite line to report a small problem at home.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

quiz_ideas: Self-check: choose the best reply to "Hoe gaat het?" (see step 7 in the lesson).
Gap-fill idea: write a sentence with **huur** in the present tense.
True/false: in Dutch yes/no questions the verb often comes before the subject.
Say one new polite request starting with **Kunt u …** or **Mag ik …**.
Paraphrase one Dutch line from the lesson input in your own English.
assessment.success_criteria: You can complete the step-7 self-check comfortably and produce at least one clear Dutch sentence about the lesson theme (spoken or written).

content_outline.writing_prompts_nl: Schrijf 5 zinnen over je buurt: wat vind je fijn, wat wil je verbeteren?
